title: Building a floating shelf with hidden brackets
subtitle: Fix invisible rod brackets into studs and slide a solid shelf over them
excerpt: How to build a floating shelf that shows no visible fixings: locating wall studs or noggins, fixing steel rod brackets into solid fixings, drilling the shelf board to accept the rods, and sliding the shelf into position.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A steel rod or rectangular bar that is fixed into the wall structure and slides into holes drilled in the back edge of a solid shelf. No fixing hardware is visible on the finished shelf; the shelf appears to float from the wall.

A vertical timber of 89 or 100 mm (3 1/2 or 4 in) width inside a plasterboard stud wall. Studs are spaced at 400 or 600 mm (16 or 24 in) centres. Invisible shelf brackets must fix into a stud or a masonry wall, not into plasterboard alone.

content.text: requires the rod fixings to go into solid structure. On a plasterboard

wall, bracket positions are dictated by the stud spacing, not by aesthetics; the shelf must be sized to span between studs rather than the other way around. On masonry walls, brackets can go anywhere after a cable-and-pipe check.
Step 1, locate fixings and scan for services

Scan the wall with a cable-and-pipe detector at the intended shelf height. Mark any detections and avoid them. Find the studs by tapping and confirming with a probe nail, or use a stud detector. Mark the stud centre-lines at shelf height. On a masonry wall, drill a test hole to confirm the wall construction; hollow-sounding plaster on masonry may be a blown section that needs re-bonding before fixing into it.
Step 2, fix the bracket rods to the wall

Use the bracket kit's template or the bracket backplate to mark the fixing holes on the wall. Drill into each stud to the depth specified by the kit (typically 65 to 80 mm / 2 1/2 to 3 in into timber). On masonry, use SDS drill and hammer mode with a carbide bit; fit a 10 mm wall plug into each hole. Screw the bracket backplates to the wall, then fit the rod or bar into the plate. Hold a long spirit level across all rods to confirm they are level before fitting the shelf.
Step 3, mark and drill the shelf board

Hold the shelf board against the wall with the back edge touching. Transfer the rod positions and heights onto the back edge with a sharp pencil. Drill each hole into the back edge with a bit matching the rod diameter, to a depth equal to the rod length minus 10 mm (3/8 in) so the shelf seats flush against the wall. Use a drill guide or depth stop to keep each hole perfectly horizontal; angled holes jam the shelf on the rods.
Step 4, slide the shelf onto the rods

Offer the shelf to the wall with the holes aligned to the rods. Push squarely back, not at an angle. The shelf should slide on in one smooth movement and seat against the wall. If it sticks halfway, remove it and check each hole depth against its rod length: a hole that is 1 mm too shallow prevents the shelf from seating. Sand or plane the back edge if the shelf face is not sitting flat against the wall.

attrs.cause: Rod brackets are spaced too widely, or the shelf board is not thick enough for the span.
attrs.fix: Maximum recommended span between rod brackets is 500 to 600 mm (20 to 24 in) for a 38 mm (1 1/2 in) thick shelf. Add a bracket at mid-span if the span is longer, or use a thicker board.

attrs.symptom: Shelf is not level after fitting
attrs.cause: One or more rods were not confirmed level before drilling the shelf.
attrs.fix: Remove the shelf. Loosen the highest rod bracket and re-set it to the correct height using the spirit level. Re-drill the shelf hole for that rod before reinstating.

attrs.symptom: Wall plug pulls out when the shelf is loaded
attrs.cause: Brackets were fixed into plasterboard alone rather than into a stud or masonry.
attrs.fix: Remove the shelf and brackets, fill the holes, and relocate brackets to coincide with stud positions. Use a stud detector to map stud layout before re-drilling.
